skill.name: yescan-universal
skill.description: Use Quark Scan through the yescan CLI for OCR, image translation, image enhancement, document conversion, and ID photo generation from user-provided images.
skill.summary: Process user-provided images with Quark Scan OCR, enhancement, translation, conversion, and ID photo workflows.
skill.author: yescan-ai

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, credential, filesystem, sensitive-data, weak-crypto, and obfuscation patterns. Manual review confirms real medium operational risk from yescan CLI execution, third-party image upload, API key handling, and temp-file outputs, while dismissing the critical heuristic, weak-crypto, Windows SAM, Ruby backtick, and entropy alerts as Markdown documentation false positives. No prompt-injection attempt or confirmed malicious intent was found.

medium_findings.title: Third-Party Image Upload and Credential Transmission
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill explicitly sends user-provided images to scan-business.quark.cn and sends SCAN_WEBSERVICE_KEY as an authentication credential. This is required for the stated OCR and image processing purpose, but it exposes sensitive image content and a credential to a third-party service.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The privacy, implementation, and security files all state the outbound image upload and API key transmission. The behavior is disclosed and purpose-aligned, but the data exposure is real.

medium_findings.title: External CLI Installation and Command Execution
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill instructs the agent to install or upgrade the yescan package and execute yescan commands. This creates supply-chain and command-execution risk, although the commands are documented and tied to the skill purpose.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The execution steps are explicit and repeated in the skill contract and README. I found no shell metacharacter injection pattern beyond documented CLI usage.

medium_findings.title: Persistent API Key Storage in User Home Directory
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill documents persistent storage of SCAN_WEBSERVICE_KEY in ~/.yescan/config.json and allows environment-variable use. This is a normal configuration pattern, but it requires users to protect and rotate the credential.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files directly describe the credential name, config command, home-directory config path, and environment variable fallback. The risk is credential handling, not evidence of exfiltration beyond the documented API request.

medium_findings.title: Temporary Output Files May Contain Sensitive Data
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill writes generated images, translated images, and converted documents to temporary directories or a user-selected output directory. These outputs may contain sensitive image content and are not documented as automatically cleaned.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation states the exact temp-directory behavior and notes that outputs are not auto-cleaned. The risk depends on the sensitivity of the processed image.

low_findings.title: Static Pattern Findings Largely From Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The Ruby backtick, weak cryptography, Windows SAM, and high-entropy alerts are not supported by semantic context. The files are Markdown documentation with command examples, file names, URLs, and descriptive text; I found no Ruby code, Windows SAM access, encrypted payload, hidden executable, or prompt-injection instruction.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review showed documentation rather than executable source for these alerts. The apparent matches come from Markdown code fences, filenames, and security-policy wording.

dangerous_patterns.title: Third-Party Image Upload and Credential Transmission
dangerous_patterns.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill explicitly sends user-provided images to scan-business.quark.cn and sends SCAN_WEBSERVICE_KEY as an authentication credential. This is required for the stated OCR and image processing purpose, but it exposes sensitive image content and a credential to a third-party service.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The privacy, implementation, and security files all state the outbound image upload and API key transmission. The behavior is disclosed and purpose-aligned, but the data exposure is real.

content.user_title: Process Images with Quark Scan OCR
content.value_statement: Image-heavy workflows often need OCR, cleanup, translation, and document conversion across many formats. This skill routes user-provided images through the yescan CLI and Quark Scan service with explicit setup and verification steps.
actual_capabilities: Lists available yescan scenes and their configurable parameters before execution.
Runs OCR scenes for IDs, invoices, tables, handwriting, formulas, and general text.
Enhances images by removing watermarks, shadows, handwriting, backgrounds, or screen artifacts when supported by the CLI.
Converts image inputs into Word, Excel, or PDF outputs through documented yescan scenes.
Generates or processes ID photo outputs using the available AIGC scene.
Verifies CLI response codes and reports text results or output file paths to the user.
limitations: Requires installing or upgrading the yescan CLI with pip3 before use.
Requires a SCAN_WEBSERVICE_KEY and sends image content to the Quark Scan service.
Only supports user-provided image files or HTTP and HTTPS image URLs up to 5 MB.
Does not support video processing, pure text editing, or direct base64 input.

use_cases.title: Extract Text From Business Images
use_cases.description: Turn photos of invoices, IDs, forms, handwritten notes, or tables into readable text or structured output.

use_cases.title: Prepare Cleaner Document Images
use_cases.description: Improve scanned images by removing marks, shadows, handwriting, or distortion before sharing or archiving.

use_cases.title: Convert Image-Based Documents
use_cases.description: Create Word, Excel, or PDF files from image inputs when the yescan scene supports the target format.

prompt_templates.title: Run Basic OCR
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for text extraction from one image.
prompt_templates.prompt: Extract the text from this image with yescan. Show me the recognized content and mention any uncertain parts.

prompt_templates.title: Clean an Image
prompt_templates.prompt: Use yescan to improve this scanned image. Choose the best available enhancement scene, then return the output file path.

prompt_templates.title: Convert a Table Photo
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert this photo of a table into an Excel document with yescan. Verify the command result before giving me the file path.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Multi-Step Pipeline
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow that chains enhancement and OCR.
prompt_templates.prompt: First enhance this low-quality document image with yescan, then run OCR on the enhanced output. Stop if any step fails.

output_examples.input: A user provides a photo of a receipt and asks for OCR.
output_examples.output: The assistant checks yescan setup, selects the matching OCR scene, runs the command, and returns the extracted receipt text.

output_examples.input: A user provides a blurry scanned form and asks for cleanup.
output_examples.output: The assistant selects an enhancement scene, processes the image, verifies success, and gives the local output file path.

output_examples.input: A user provides an image of a spreadsheet and asks for Excel conversion.
output_examples.output: The assistant discovers the conversion scene, runs it on the image, and reports the generated spreadsheet path.

best_practices: Confirm that the user understands image content will be sent to Quark Scan before processing sensitive files.
Use the scene list from the current CLI session instead of assuming scene names or parameters.
Clean temporary output directories after processing confidential or regulated images.
anti_patterns: Do not process files or URLs that the user did not explicitly provide in the current request.
Do not fabricate yescan scene names, response fields, or command results.
Do not paste API keys into public logs, screenshots, examples, or shared transcripts.

faq.question: What does this skill process?
faq.answer: It processes user-provided images for OCR, translation, enhancement, document conversion, and ID photo generation.

faq.question: Does it send data to an external service?
faq.answer: Yes. It sends the selected image and API credential to the Quark Scan service for processing.

faq.question: Does it need an API key?
faq.answer: Yes. The yescan CLI needs SCAN_WEBSERVICE_KEY configured through its config command or an environment variable.

faq.question: Can it process local files?
faq.answer: Yes. It can process a local image path that the user explicitly provides.

faq.question: Can it process image URLs?
faq.answer: Yes. It supports HTTP and HTTPS image URLs when the URL is provided by the user.

faq.question: Can it handle videos or plain text?
faq.answer: No. The documented scope is image input, not video processing or pure text editing.
